abstract: In natural photosynthesis, the solar-to-chemical energy conversion process begins with the light-driven splitting of water into electrons, protons, and molecular oxygen. This reaction is catalyzed by the $\text{Mn}_4\text{CaO}_5$ cluster within photosystem II. To drive O–O bond formation, the cluster sequentially accumulates four oxidizing equivalents—represented by the $S_0$ through $S_4$ intermediate states of the Kok cycle—driven by photochemical charge separations in the reaction center. Here, we employ room-temperature serial femtosecond X-ray crystallography to capture structural snapshots of the final step in this cycle: the $S_3 \rightarrow [S_4] \rightarrow S_0$ transition, during which oxygen is evolved and the catalytic clock is reset. Our results uncover a sophisticated sequence of events unfolding over micro- to millisecond timescales. These include structural rearrangements of the $\text{Mn}_4\text{CaO}_5$ cluster and its ligands, shifts in water-access pathways, and the coordinated release of protons through the Cl1 hydrogen-bonding channel. Notably, the additional oxygen atom ($O_x$), introduced as a bridging ligand between Ca and Mn1 during the $S_2 \rightarrow S_3$ transition, disappears or relocates approximately 700 µs after the third flash, coinciding with the reduction of $Y_Z$. The onset of $O_2$ evolution is marked by a shortening of the Mn1–Mn4 distance at roughly 1,200 µs, indicating the formation of a reduced intermediate, potentially a bound peroxide, prior to the restoration of the $S_0$ state.
